goldenComments: tokenStore.put is called with (userId, token, cacheTtlSeconds) but the store expects (key, ttlSeconds, value) — argument order is wrong and will store the TTL as the token value.
No size bound on the denial cache allows an attacker to exhaust memory with random resource names.

findings.label: bug
findings.suggestionContent: WHAT: tokenStore.put is called with (userId, token, cacheTtlSeconds). WHY: The store signature is put(key, ttlSeconds, value), so the token is stored as the TTL and the real TTL becomes the value. HOW: Call tokenStore.put(userId, cacheTtlSeconds, token).

findings.label: bug
findings.suggestionContent: WHAT: Arguments to tokenStore.put are in the wrong order. WHY: This corrupts the cache entry. HOW: Swap token and cacheTtlSeconds.

findings.label: performance
findings.oneSentenceSummary: Missing log on cache write failure
findings.suggestionContent: WHAT: put() swallows redis errors. WHY: Operators cannot diagnose write failures. HOW: Log the error before rethrowing.
findings.improvedCode: try { await redis.set(key, value, ttlSeconds); } catch (e) { logger.error(e); throw e; }
